question: Who is the author of the play 'On The Face Of It'?
explanation: The play 'On The Face Of It' is written by Susan Hill, as mentioned at the beginning of the text.
subject: English

question: What is the main physical impairment of Mr Lamb?
explanation: Mr Lamb has a tin leg because his real one got blown off in a war years back.
subject: English

question: What caused the severe disfigurement on Derry's face?
explanation: Derry states, "I got acid all down that side of my face and it burned it all away. It ate my face up."
subject: English

question: How does Derry enter Mr Lamb's garden in Scene One?
options: He walks through the open front gate
He climbs over the garden wall
He is invited in by Mr Lamb
explanation: Mr Lamb points out that the gate is always open, to which he adds, "Only you climbed the garden wall."
subject: English

question: What does Mr Lamb immediately warn Derry to mind when he enters the garden?
explanation: Mr Lamb's first words to Derry are, "Mind the apples!" referring to the windfalls of crab apples in the long grass.
subject: English

question: Why did Derry originally enter the garden?
options: To hide from the neighborhood kids
Because he thought it was an empty place
Because he wanted to meet Mr Lamb
explanation: Derry tells Mr Lamb, "I thought this was an empty place. I didn't know there was anybody here...."
subject: English

question: How old is Derry?
explanation: When Mr Lamb guesses Derry is "Thirteen or so," Derry corrects him and says, "Fourteen."
subject: English

question: What do some of the neighborhood kids call Mr Lamb?
explanation: Mr Lamb tells Derry, "Lamey-Lamb, some kids say. Haven't you heard them? You will. Lamey-Lamb."
subject: English

question: What is Mr Lamb's philosophical view regarding 'weeds' and 'flowers'?
options: Weeds are pests that must be destroyed
Flowers are inherently more valuable than weeds
Both are green, growing plants and it's all life
Weeds are only useful for making jelly
explanation: Mr Lamb says, "Why is one green, growing plant called a weed and another 'flower'? Where's the difference. It's all life.... growing."
subject: English

question: What does Mr Lamb make out of the ripe crab apples?
explanation: Mr Lamb mentions the crab apples and says, "They're ripe for it. I make jelly."
subject: English

question: Why does Derry feel upset about how his mother kisses him?
options: She kisses him too often in public
She kisses him on the other side of his face because she has to
She refuses to kiss him at all
She kisses him on his burned cheek out of pity
explanation: Derry says, "Only my mother, and she kisses me on the other side of my face, and I don't like my mother to kiss me, she does it because she has to."
subject: English

question: What fairy tale does Derry reference when talking about inner beauty?
explanation: Derry references the story of Beauty and the Beast, saying "Beauty loved the monstrous beast for himself and when she kissed him he changed into a handsome prince."
subject: English

question: What cruel remark did Derry overhear a woman say about him at a bus stop?
options: 'That's a face only a mother could love.'
explanation: Derry recounts hearing a woman whisper, "Look at that, that's a terrible thing. That's a face only a mother could love."
subject: English

question: According to Mr Lamb, what is worse than a bottle of acid burning your face?
options: Locking yourself in a room forever
Hating people and burning yourself away inside
explanation: When Derry says he hates some people, Mr Lamb replies, "That'd do you more harm than any bottle of acid. Acid only burns your face... You can burn yourself away inside."
subject: English

question: What is the story of the man who locked himself up in his room, as told by Mr Lamb?
options: He was hiding from the police
He was afraid of everything in the world and eventually a picture fell on his head and killed him
He was badly burned in a fire and refused to show his face
He hated people and died of a broken heart
explanation: Mr Lamb tells the story of a man who was afraid of everything and locked himself in his room, only to be killed when a picture fell off the wall onto his head.
subject: English

question: What is Derry's actual given name?
explanation: Derry reveals his name saying, "Derry. Only it's Derek....but I hate that. Derry."
subject: English

question: Why doesn't Mr Lamb have curtains at his windows?
options: He likes the light and the darkness, and to hear the wind
He wants people to look inside and visit
They remind him of the hospital
explanation: Mr Lamb says, "I'm not fond of curtains. Shutting things out, shutting things in. I like the light and the darkness, and the windows open, to hear the wind."
subject: English

question: What sound in nature does Derry say he likes to hear?
explanation: After Mr Lamb talks about hearing the wind, Derry agrees and says, "When it's raining, I like to hear it on the roof."
subject: English

question: How does Mr Lamb perceive the sound of his bees?
options: He hears them humming, which to him means 'singing'
He thinks they sound like a loud motor
explanation: Mr Lamb says, "Some hear bees and they say, bees buzz. But when you listen to bees for a long while, they humm....and hum means 'sing'. I hear them singing, my bees."
subject: English

question: What cruel thing did Derry overhear his parents discussing downstairs?
options: How they could afford his medical bills
Whether they should send him to a special school
'What's going to happen to him when we've gone?'
That they wished he had never been born
explanation: Derry overheard them saying, "What'll he ever do? What's going to happen to him when we've gone? How ever will he get on in this world? Looking like that?"
subject: English

question: How does Mr Lamb respond to Derry's fears about his future?
options: He agrees that Derry will struggle immensely
He points out Derry has two arms, legs, eyes, a tongue, and a brain, and can get on better than the rest
He offers to adopt Derry and take care of him
He tells Derry to become a gardener like him
explanation: Mr Lamb says, "Lord, boy, you've got two arms, two legs and eyes and ears, you've got a tongue and a brain. You'll get on the way you want, like all the rest."
subject: English

question: According to Derry, someone told him he'd be 'better off' where?
options: With others like himself, such as in the hospital
Living far away in the countryside
explanation: Derry recounts, "After I'd come home, one person said, 'He'd have been better off stopping in there. In the hospital. He'd be better off with others like himself.'"
subject: English

question: What does Mr Lamb make for the kids who come to his garden?
explanation: Mr Lamb says, "Kids come for the apples and pears. And for toffee. I make toffee with honey."
subject: English

question: How does Mr Lamb justify not needing to know someone's name or particulars before considering them a friend?
options: He says memory fails him anyway
He says he doesn't need to put them in a filing box to be a friend
He says names are just illusions
He says true friends communicate without words
explanation: Mr Lamb says, "Why should that signify? Do I have to write all your particulars down and put them in a filing box, before you can be a friend?"
subject: English

question: When Derry claims that just passing someone in the street doesn't mean they're friends, what is Mr Lamb's counter-argument?
options: It doesn't mean they're enemies, either
Enemies are just friends you haven't made yet
explanation: Mr Lamb responds to Derry's statement by saying, "Doesn't mean they're enemies, either, does it?"
subject: English

question: What three activities does Mr Lamb say he uses to 'make all that out' (understand the world)?
explanation: When Derry asks how he makes all that out, Mr Lamb replies, "Watching. Listening. Thinking."
subject: English

question: How far away does Derry live from Mr Lamb's house?
explanation: Derry says, "It's three miles home, across the fields."
subject: English

question: Why does Derry feel he must leave the garden before it gets too late?
options: He is afraid of the dark
His mother will want to know where he is
He needs to do his homework
explanation: Derry explains his need to leave by saying, "But my mother'll want to know where I am. It's three miles home... but they still want to know where I am."
subject: English

question: What does Derry claim will happen if he goes home to tell his mother where he is?
options: She will come back with him
She will never let him come back
She will invite Mr Lamb over for dinner
explanation: Derry tells Mr Lamb, "Once I got home, they'd never let me come back."
subject: English

question: What is Mr Lamb's theory about why Derry wouldn't return if he went home?
options: His mother would lock him in
Derry would be too lazy to walk three miles again
Once he got home, Derry would never let HIMSELF come back
explanation: Mr Lamb counters Derry's claim by saying, "Once you got home, you'd never let yourself come back."
subject: English

question: What does Derry say he ultimately wants in life?
options: Something no one else has got or ever will have, something just his
To own a big house and a garden like Mr Lamb's
explanation: Derry expresses his frustration by saying, "I want....something no one else has got or ever will have. Something just mine. Like this garden."
subject: English

question: Before Derry runs home, what does he promise Mr Lamb?
options: That he will never bother him again
That he will send his mother to speak to him
That he will bring money for the apples
explanation: Derry shouts as he is leaving, "I'm going. But I'll come back. You see. You wait. I can run. I haven't got a tin leg. I'll be back."
subject: English

question: After Derry runs off in Scene One, what does Mr Lamb say to himself?
options: 'They never do come back. Not them.'
'I should have given him some apples.'
explanation: Mr Lamb talks to himself and the bees, saying, "They never do, though. Not them. Never do come back."
subject: English

question: In Scene Two, how long does Derry's mother say they have lived in their current house?
explanation: Derry's mother says, "We've not lived here three months, but I know what there is to know and you're not to go back there."
subject: English

question: What is Derry's mother's opinion of Mr Lamb?
options: She thinks he is a kind, lonely old man
She is afraid of him and warns Derry to stay away based on what she has heard
She thinks he is a relative they shouldn't speak to
She believes he is a doctor who can cure Derry
explanation: She warns Derry strongly, saying she has "been told. Warned," and insists that Derry is "not to go back there."
subject: English

question: Why does Derry tell his mother he wants to go back to Mr Lamb's garden?
options: To hide from the neighborhood bullies
To sit, look, and listen to things that matter
To get free apples and toffee
explanation: Derry tells his mother he wants to "sit and....listen to things. Listen and look... Things that matter. Things nobody else has ever said."
subject: English

question: How does Derry respond when his mother says, 'You're best off here'?
options: He agrees and goes to his room
He ignores her and packs a bag
He asks her to come with him to the garden
explanation: When his mother tells him he is best off there, Derry immediately replies, "I hate it here."
subject: English

question: What does Derry realize is NOT important anymore during the argument with his mother?
options: His face and what he looks like
explanation: Derry passionately states, "It's got nothing to do with my face and what I look like. I don't care about that and it isn't important."
subject: English

question: What drastic statement does Derry make right before he runs out of his house in Scene Two?
options: "If I don't go back there, I'll never go anywhere in this world again."
"I wish I had died in the accident."
explanation: Derry tells his mother, "Because if I don't go back there, I'll never go anywhere in this world again," demonstrating how crucial the connection with Mr Lamb has become.
subject: English

question: At the beginning of Scene Three, what action is Mr Lamb engaged in?
options: Reading a book in the sun
Pulling down crab apples with a ladder and a stick
explanation: The scene opens with garden sounds of a branch shifting and apples thumping down, and Mr Lamb says, "Steady....that's....got it," indicating he is on the ladder pulling down apples as he planned.
subject: English

question: What event occurs just before Derry opens the garden gate in Scene Three?
options: A creak, a crash, and the ladder falls back with Mr Lamb
explanation: The stage directions note: "[A creak. A crash. The ladder falls back, Mr Lamb with it. A thump... Then silence.]"
subject: English

question: What are Derry's first words when he returns to the garden?
options: "You see, you see! I came back."
explanation: Derry enters panting and says, "You see, you see! I came back. You said I wouldn't and they said....but I came back..."
subject: English

question: What is the implied condition of Mr Lamb at the end of the play?
options: He is laughing on the grass
He is dead or severely injured from the fall
He has walked away to the beehives
explanation: Mr Lamb falls with the ladder, there is a "crash" and a "thump", followed by "silence". When Derry finds him, he gets no response, implying Mr Lamb is dead or unconscious/severely injured.
subject: English

question: What emotional reaction does Derry have at the very end of the play?
options: He gets angry and kicks the ladder
explanation: The final stage direction for Derry is: "[Derry begins to weep.]"
subject: English

question: What affectionate/defiant nickname does Derry use for Mr Lamb at the very end?
explanation: As Derry weeps over Mr Lamb's body, he says, "I came back. Lamey-Lamb. I did.....come back."
subject: English

question: In Mr Lamb's view, how do children react to him over time?
options: They never stop being terrified of him
They eventually stop staring and get tired of it
They refuse to ever speak to him
explanation: When Derry says people will stare at his leg, Mr Lamb says, "Some do. Some don't. They get tired of it, in the end. There's plenty of other things to stare at."
subject: English

question: What does Derry complain that people always say about individuals in pain?
options: That they should complain more loudly
That they are brave and never cry or feel sorry for themselves
explanation: Derry complains: "Oh, that's something else they all say. 'Look at all those people who are in pain and brave and never cry and never complain and don't feel sorry for themselves.'"
subject: English

question: When Derry asks Mr Lamb 'Aren't you interested?', what is Derry referring to?
explanation: Derry expects Mr Lamb to ask about his burned face like everyone else does. When Mr Lamb changes the subject to apples, Derry is surprised and asks, "Aren't you interested?"
subject: English

question: Why does Derry think that even when he is as old as Mr Lamb, he will still look the same?
options: Because he takes very good care of his skin
Because he will still only have 'half a face'
Because acid burns make you look younger
Because he plans to get plastic surgery
explanation: Derry laments, "When I'm as old as you, I'll look the same. I'll still only have half a face."
subject: English

question: What is Mr Lamb's response when Derry asks him to name some of the friends who visit him?
options: He says he can't remember their names
He says 'What are names? Tom, Dick or Harry.'
He lists out five distinct names of local children
explanation: When Derry challenges Mr Lamb to name his friends, Mr Lamb casually replies, "What are names? Tom, Dick or Harry."
subject: English
